result:
I’ve been thinking about hands lately. Not in the poetic, “hands are the window to the soul” way, but as a quiet chronicle of everything we never bother to say out loud. My grandmother’s hands, for instance, were topographical maps: ridged with veins like rivers, knuckles swollen into low mountain ranges, skin so thin it looked backlit by memory. When she kneaded dough for Sunday bread, her fingers moved with the same certainty they’d used to change my mother’s diapers, to plant beans, to smuggle extra cucumbers from the community garden into her purse. Each push and fold of dough was a sentence in a private language I never learned to read. I only knew the translation because the room smelled of yeast and forgiveness.

Hands remember even when the mind checks out. My father’s left hand developed a tremor the year he turned sixty-three—a tiny, persistent vibration that made his coffee cup chatter against the saucer like false teeth. He pretended not to notice, kept up his nightly routine of sanding cedar blocks for the birdhouses he sold at the farmers’ market. The tremor worsened, but the sanding somehow grew smoother; while his neurons misfired, his palm negotiated an armistice with gravity. After he died I found a storage bin of those blocks, each one beveled so delicately they felt soft, as though they’d been carved from butter instead of wood. I held one and realized I was touching the negative space of his illness: the steadiness he stole back from entropy.

We underestimate how much history is stored in the creases. A friend who works as a crime-scene cleaner once told me that no matter how meticulous the crew, there’s always a thumbnail-size smudge on a light switch or a half-print on the back of a nightstand drawer. “People think DNA is fancy,” she said, “but mostly we leave ourselves behind in grease.” She keeps a bottle of citrus solvent in her glove compartment and swabs petrol-station pumps for fun, collecting strangers’ stories in minuscule oil paintings she’ll never decode. I asked if the residue ever bothered her. She shrugged: “I like knowing we’re impossible to erase completely.”

That thought travels with me when I ride the subway. I look along the aluminum rail and see a mosaic of partial fingerprints, like a pointillist portrait of the city. Somewhere in that constellation is the woman who cried into her mask last Tuesday, the kid who just discovered he can balance on the seat backs, the courier who delivered my asthma meds during the first lockdown. Together our prints form an invisible coral reef, layer upon layer of calcium carbonate memory accreting in the dark.

Of course, hands are also weapons. I’ve clasped mine under fluorescent lights in hospital corridors while doctors used the word “amputate.” I’ve watched footage of hands raised at a protest become hands yanked behind backs, the moment hope pivots into evidence. Even the small betrayals: the first time I held my newborn niece and felt her miniature fingers close around my thumb, I understood how easily an entire life can be placed in someone else’s grasp without consent. She squeezed, reflexively, the way ivy clings to brick, and I felt the terrifying elasticity of responsibility.

I used to bite my nails—tiny sieges of anxiety I camouflaged with bitter polish that only made me resent the taste of myself. When I finally stopped, at thirty-one, the white crescents grew back like unfamiliar moons. For weeks I couldn’t stop staring at the neat horizon of keratin, amazed that my body would rebuild a frontier I’d spent decades tearing down. It felt like forgiveness in slow motion, a referendum on self-harm decided by cells with no investment in my drama.

Hands are time travelers. Hold them up to a mirror and you’ll meet the child who once marveled that fingers could fold into a duck or a moon-eating crocodile. Turn them palm-down and you preview the articulated skeleton you’ll eventually become, bones polite enough to pre-announce their final shape. Between those bookends, we conduct symphonies, swipe screens, change tires, perform surgeries on hearts we’ll never see. We signal hello and goodbye with the same appendage; the difference is merely a matter of orientation.

When I travel, I collect small, stupid souvenirs: ticket stubs, airline napkins, bar receipts. But the only artifacts I reliably revisit are the sensations stored in my hands. Grazing the pocked wall of the Colosseum at dawn, before security ropes tether tourists into choreographed awe. The unexpected velvet of Greek wind on skin—velvet because heat can have texture if you pay attention. The rubbery give of a baby sea urchin I wasn’t supposed to touch but did anyway, its spines kissing my palm like a reluctant orchestra of toothpicks. Try capturing that on a postcard.

Eventually everything returns to touch. Language is just touch by other means; a poem is the ghost of a hand reaching through time and finding your shoulder. I think that’s why we flinch from bad news delivered via text—no warmth of palm on wrist, no nuanced pressure that says I’m still here even while the world tilts. Digital intimacy is a brilliant forgery, but it lacks sweat and static electricity, the microscopically specific salt of a lover’s skin after a day in the sun.

I’ve been trying to practice deliberate tactility. It feels radical in an era of glass and stainless steel. I’ll eat an orange in silence, listening to the tear of peel, feeling the mist of citrus oils evaporate into my pores. I’ll walk barefoot across the apartment just to register the temperature change where the tiles meet the oak floorboards. Yesterday I spent five minutes running my thumb along the spine of a hardcover, counting the ridges of glue and thread. The book was a forgettable thriller I picked up at a yard sale, but the act transformed it into topography, a private mountain range I can climb without moving.

Sometimes I worry I’m hoarding sensation against some future drought, the way my grandmother stockpiled sugar packets in coat pockets “just in case.” Maybe all mindfulness is a kind of doomsday prepping, stacking tins of presence for the winter of distraction. But then I catch myself touching the small scar on my knee—remnant of a bicycle crash at nine—and realize memory doesn’t need stockpiling; it leaks into the present whether I invite it or not. The scar lights up when I glance at it, nerve endings performing a Shakespearean aside: Remember the gravel that melted into your skin? Remember how your mother’s voice cracked when she said you’d be okay?

I’ve written before about the importance of voice, of breath, of the stories we trade like currency. But tonight it’s hands that feel like the truest passport: visas stamped in callus, customs declarations scribbled in chapped cuticles. They carry passports to every room we’ve entered, every body we’ve craved, every grief we’ve tried to throw away but ended up re-gifting to ourselves. They are maps, yes, but they are also the roads, the vehicles, and the hitchhikers—all at once.

So here’s to the unspoken thousand words we carry at the ends of our arms. May they keep reaching, keep failing, keep rebuilding their borders. May they learn new dialects of tenderness and still find the strength to clench when necessary. May they remember, despite every advertisement promising amnesia, that the world is not a screen to be swiped but a living fabric we’re perpetually in the middle of weaving. And when the day comes that they finally fall still, may someone hold them—gently, deliberately—and read in their silence every story we never remembered to tell.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
